meta.title: Nephrology Literature Update
meta.query: Clinical nephrology — RCTs, systematic reviews & meta-analyses (humans, English)
meta.source: Generated by Google NotebookLM, grounded in PubMed (NLM) abstracts. Each entry links to its PubMed record and DOI.
meta.note: Summaries and PICO are NotebookLM analyses of the source abstracts; criteria marked “not specified” were not stated.

articles.authors: Tear AA, Fauziah N, Riswari SF, Purba AR, Akhlaq MM, et al.
articles.title: Prognostic factors in malaria patients with acute kidney injury: a systematic review and meta analysis.
articles.summary: The study identified several factors significantly associated with mortality in malaria-associated AKI, including respiratory distress syndrome (OR: 18.50, p < 0.001) and hypotension (OR: 18.23, p < 0.001). Other predictors included oliguria (OR: 3.41), hyperkalemia (OR: 3.99), and acidosis (OR: 4.88).

pico.population: Malaria patients with acute kidney injury
pico.intervention: Presence of clinical or laboratory abnormalities
pico.comparison: Absence of clinical or laboratory abnormalities

inclusion: studies showing the association between clinical or laboratory abnormalities and mortality

articles.authors: Møller M, Andersen TH, Borg R, Christensen R, Bressendorff I, et al.
articles.title: Complication rates of 16- and 18-gauge needles for native kidney biopsies: a systematic review and proportional meta-analysis.
articles.summary: Pooled major complication rates were 1.83% (95% CI: 1.20-2.79) for 16G needles and 1.29% (95% CI: 0.78-2.13) for 18G needles, with no statistically significant difference between them. Mean glomerular yield was similar at 18.8 for 16G and 17.5 for 18G needles.

pico.outcome: Major complication rates and diagnostic yield

articles.authors: Hu D, Chen L, Mai R, Gao T, Guan B.
articles.title: Bibliometric analysis of SGLT2 inhibitor treatment for diabetic kidney disease.
articles.summary: Analysis of 2,415 articles from 2013-2025 demonstrated exponential growth in research regarding SGLT2 inhibitors and diabetic kidney disease. The research focus has evolved from clinical trial design to cardiorenal protective mechanisms and precision disease management.

pico.population: Published articles regarding SGLT2 inhibitors and diabetic kidney disease
pico.intervention: Bibliometric analysis using CiteSpace and VOSviewer
pico.outcome: Identification of research trends, key institutions, and core directions

inclusion: articles from Web of Science Core Collection
relevant to SGLT2 inhibitor treatment for diabetic kidney disease

articles.authors: Chichelero GM, Pessil LS, Viegas GM, Xavier Junior GF, Dos Reis MA, et al.
articles.title: Prevalence of diabetic kidney disease in Brazil: A systematic review with meta-analysis.
articles.summary: The pooled prevalence of DKD among Brazilian adults was 27.06% (95% CI 22.40-32.29). Longer diabetes duration (≥10 years) was associated with a higher prevalence of 33.28% compared to 24.32% for those with shorter durations.

pico.outcome: Pooled prevalence of diabetic kidney disease

inclusion: studies published in PubMed, EMBASE, and LILACS
studies up to and including December 2025
reporting prevalence of DKD in Brazil

articles.authors: Kelly DM, Kelleher EM, Rothwell PM.
articles.title: Acute Kidney Injury and Risk of Adverse Neurocognitive Outcomes: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.summary: AKI was consistently associated with increased risk of stroke (pooled adjusted HR 1.35, 95% CI 1.20-1.52), delirium (pooled adjusted OR 1.76; 1.42-2.17), and dementia (pooled adjusted HR 1.64, 1.41-1.89). Furthermore, AKI was linked to higher in-hospital mortality (pooled HR 2.13) and 90-day mortality (pooled HR 4.81) following stroke.

pico.population: Adult populations with a history of acute kidney injury
pico.outcome: Risk of stroke, delirium, and dementia

inclusion: quantitative estimates with measures of precision
association between AKI and delirium, stroke, or dementia

articles.authors: Costa TA, de Sousa LMT, Nogueira A, Chagas GCL, Abreu M, et al.
articles.title: Association between SGLT2 inhibitors and outcomes after heart transplant in patients with type 2 diabetes: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.summary: SGLT2 inhibitor use in heart transplant recipients with T2D was associated with a lower risk of all-cause mortality (aHR 0.69; 95% CI 0.51-0.93) compared with non-use. No increased risk of urinary tract infection was observed (OR 0.77; 95% CI 0.14-4.05), and data suggested stable or increased eGFR.

pico.population: Heart transplant recipients with type 2 diabetes
pico.outcome: All-cause mortality, urinary tract infection (UTI), and eGFR change

inclusion: studies of HTX recipients with T2D
reported outcomes including mortality, UTI, or eGFR change

articles.authors: Gou Y, Huang W, Liang M.
articles.title: Efficacy and safety of traditional Chinese medicine in treating pediatric IgA vasculitis nephritis: A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.
articles.summary: TCM interventions significantly improved the overall clinical effective rate (RR = 1.20, 95% CI [1.15-1.25]) and reduced 24-hour urinary protein excretion (SMD = -0.83) compared with Western medicine alone. There was no significant difference in the incidence of adverse events between the two groups (RR = 0.92).

pico.intervention: Traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) interventions (alone or combined)
pico.outcome: Overall clinical effective rate and 24-hour urinary protein excretion

inclusion: comparing TCM with Western medicine alone
studies from eight databases searched until March 2025

articles.authors: Dos Santos Borges R, Lara Santos R, Haikal de Paula L, Borges Martins JM, Machado Gomes de Sousa PA, et al.
articles.title: Efficacy, safety, and biomarker changes of B-cell activating factor and A proliferation-inducing ligand-targeted therapies in IgA nephropathy: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.
articles.summary: Targeted therapies against BAFF or APRIL significantly reduced 24-hour UPCR by a mean difference of -38.94% (95% CI -58.98 to -18.90) and improved eGFR by 7.05 mL/min/1.73m². These treatments also led to significant reductions in serum Gd-IgA1, IgG, IgA, and IgM levels.

pico.outcome: Mean percent change in 24-hour UPCR and mean change in eGFR

inclusion: comparing BAFF- or APRIL-targeted drugs with placebo
